cases.title: The Missing Ledger
cases.intro: A senior accountant at Hartley and Sons was found dead at his desk on a Monday morning. The company financial ledger had vanished from his locked office. Three people had after-hours access to the building that night.

suspects.name: Diana Marsh
suspects.description: Junior accountant, passed over for promotion twice this year

suspects.name: Gerald Thorn
suspects.description: Night security guard with a documented gambling problem

suspects.name: Claire Sutton
suspects.description: Senior partner's personal assistant, held a duplicate office key

clues: The office door was locked from the inside when found. The window latch was unlatched.
Security logs show Gerald Thorn clocking out at 11:47 PM, two hours before the estimated time of death.
Claire Sutton's keycard records show access to the server room at 1:15 AM, though she claims to have been home all night.
Diana Marsh has no keycard activity recorded after 10:00 PM.
cases.explanation: Gerald's alibi is confirmed by the security log. Diana had no after-hours keycard activity. Claire's keycard placed her inside the building during the estimated time of death, directly contradicting her alibi. The unlatched window explains how she exited a room locked from the inside.
cases.hint: Check who was provably inside the building after midnight.

cases.title: The Broken Safe
cases.intro: A jewelry store owner was found unconscious beside his cracked safe at closing time. Around 40,000 in stones were gone. The store had been locked all day with no signs of forced entry from outside.

suspects.name: Ray Kowalski
suspects.description: Store manager, knew the combination and had a key to the back door

suspects.name: Tara Bloom
suspects.description: Part-time cashier, recently asked for a pay advance and was refused

suspects.name: Sol Navarro
suspects.description: Delivery contractor who made a scheduled afternoon drop that day

clues: The back door was unlocked from the inside. The front deadbolt was engaged.
Sol Navarro signed out at 3:40 PM. The dock camera confirms he left the building at that time.
Tara Bloom called in sick that morning and did not enter the store at any point during the day.
The safe shows no tool marks or drilling. It was opened by someone who knew the combination.
cases.explanation: Sol was confirmed off-site hours before the theft window. Tara never entered the building. The absence of tool marks means the safe was opened with the combination, not forced. Ray was the only person present who knew the code and was unaccounted for during the relevant window.
cases.hint: A safe with no tool marks was opened by someone who already knew the code.

cases.title: The Last Train
cases.intro: A businessman was found dead in a first-class compartment on the 11:40 PM overnight train from Caldwell. The train made no stops between departure and discovery. Three other passengers occupied nearby seats.

suspects.name: Nora Vance
suspects.description: Retired nurse traveling to visit family, seated directly across the aisle

suspects.name: Phillip Osei
suspects.description: Sales representative, seated directly behind the victim, a frequent rider on this route

suspects.name: Marta Frei
suspects.description: Academic returning from a conference, seated two rows ahead facing the rear

clues: The victim was poisoned. A fast-acting compound was introduced to his drink.
The conductor observed Nora Vance sleeping in her seat from 11:55 PM until the body was discovered.
Marta Frei's rear-facing seat made reaching the victim's drink impossible without passing Nora Vance, who was awake at the relevant time.
Phillip Osei ordered two drinks from the trolley. The victim's receipt shows only one drink ordered.
cases.explanation: Nora's alibi is confirmed by the conductor. Marta's seat position made access physically implausible without passing an alert witness. Phillip ordered two drinks when the victim ordered one, pointing to an additional drink used as a vehicle for the compound introduced during his time standing in the aisle.
cases.hint: Someone was standing near the victim's drink with an unexplained extra order.

cases.title: The Gallery Fire
cases.intro: A fire broke out overnight in a private art gallery in the Kellerton district. Two paintings worth a combined 200,000 were missing when firefighters cleared the building. The fire was set deliberately to mask the theft.

suspects.name: Owen Farris
suspects.description: Gallery manager, had a master key and had recently taken out a personal loan

suspects.name: Simone Greer
suspects.description: Freelance restorer who had worked in the gallery three days prior

suspects.name: Marcus Boyle
suspects.description: Security contractor responsible for the gallery's alarm system

clues: The fire originated in the storage room, which only Owen Farris and the gallery owner had keys to.
Marcus Boyle's system logs show the alarm was manually disarmed at 2:08 AM using a four-digit override code known only to Boyle and the owner.
Simone Greer's access badge had not been activated since her last legitimate visit three days ago.
Owen Farris told police he was home at 2:08 AM, but a toll camera recorded his vehicle four blocks from the gallery at 1:58 AM.
cases.explanation: Simone had no access that night. Marcus could disarm the alarm but had no key to the storage room where the fire originated. Owen had both the storage key and was placed four blocks away ten minutes before the alarm was disarmed, contradicting his alibi.
cases.hint: Two things had to happen: someone had a key to the storage room and a way to kill the alarm.

cases.title: The Forged Will
cases.intro: An elderly widow died and left her estate to a local charity. Three days later, a second will surfaced, redirecting the estate to a distant relative. A document examiner confirmed the second will was forged.

suspects.name: Harriet Cole
suspects.description: The deceased's housekeeper of twelve years, left nothing in the original will

suspects.name: Douglas Vane
suspects.description: Distant relative who stands to inherit under the forged document

suspects.name: Lena Morrow
suspects.description: Estate solicitor who handled both documents

clues: The forged will was printed on paper stock that matches a brand sold exclusively at a supply shop in Douglas Vane's hometown, 200 miles away.
Lena Morrow's office records show the original will was sealed and filed three months before the death. She had no contact with Douglas Vane before the death.
Harriet Cole has no record of travel, no computer at home, and no connection to the paper supplier.
Douglas Vane flew into the city two days before the widow's death and checked into a hotel four streets from the solicitor's office.
cases.explanation: Harriet had no means of sourcing the specific paper or printing the document. Lena's records show she filed the original will legitimately and had no prior contact with Vane. The paper stock traces directly to Vane's hometown, and his unexplained visit to the city immediately before the death placed him in position to plant the document.
cases.hint: The paper the forgery was printed on narrows the field considerably.

cases.title: The Riverside Club
cases.intro: A member of an exclusive private club was found dead in the billiards room after the evening's social concluded. The room had been locked for the final hour of the event. Three members had been seen in the corridor near the room during that hour.

suspects.name: Edmund Voss
suspects.description: Retired banker, had a public dispute with the victim over a debt the week before

suspects.name: Clara Nighton
suspects.description: Club treasurer, recently discovered the victim had been embezzling from club funds

suspects.name: Peter Ashwick
suspects.description: The club's junior steward, responsible for locking and unlocking all rooms that evening

clues: The victim was struck from behind with a billiards cue. No struggle is evident; the victim did not see it coming.
Peter Ashwick locked the billiards room at 9:30 PM per the event log and did not reopen it until the body was found.
Edmund Voss was observed by two independent witnesses in the main lounge from 9:15 PM to 10:40 PM.
Clara Nighton left the main event at 9:20 PM, told staff she had a headache, and was not seen again until 10:45 PM.
cases.explanation: Peter locked the room and did not reopen it, and he had no apparent motive. Edmund has a confirmed alibi from two witnesses for the entire window. Clara disappeared from the event at 9:20 PM, ten minutes before the room was locked, and cannot account for her whereabouts during that time. Her discovery of the embezzlement gives her a strong motive.
cases.hint: Focus on who was unaccounted for during the hour the room was sealed.

cases.title: The Poisoned Thermos
cases.intro: A logistics manager collapsed during a Monday morning team meeting after drinking from his personal thermos. Toxicology confirmed a slow-acting poison. The thermos had been in his unlocked office over the weekend.

suspects.name: Janet Rao
suspects.description: Direct report who had filed a formal complaint against the manager two weeks prior

suspects.name: Karl Severin
suspects.description: IT technician who serviced equipment in the manager's office on Saturday

suspects.name: Paula Dent
suspects.description: Office cleaner who worked the Sunday evening shift

clues: Building access logs show Janet Rao did not badge into the building at any point over the weekend.
The poison requires refrigeration to remain stable. The thermos was found at room temperature with no refrigeration access in the office.
Karl Severin's work order shows he was in the manager's office Saturday morning. He has a kitchen with a refrigerator at his on-site maintenance station.
Paula Dent's cleaning log does not include the manager's office; her assigned section was the third floor, not the second.
cases.explanation: Janet never entered the building that weekend. Paula's assigned area excluded the manager's office. The poison's requirement for refrigeration rules out anyone without cold storage access. Karl was physically in the office Saturday morning and has the only refrigeration source that could keep the compound stable before application.
cases.hint: The poison had a cold-chain requirement. Not everyone had access to refrigeration.

cases.title: The Vanishing Witness
cases.intro: A key witness in a fraud trial disappeared the night before she was scheduled to testify. Her apartment showed no signs of struggle. Three people knew her location under the protection program.

suspects.name: Detective Marsh
suspects.description: Lead investigator on the fraud case, had full access to the protection details

suspects.name: Sara Kline
suspects.description: Victim advocate assigned to the witness, only remaining program contact

suspects.name: Victor Holst
suspects.description: Defense attorney for the accused, known to have paid for information in a prior case

clues: The protection location was shared internally via an encrypted memo. Access logs show only Detective Marsh and Sara Kline accessed the memo.
Victor Holst had no documented access to any protection program records.
Sara Kline's phone records show a 14-minute call to an unregistered prepaid number at 11:20 PM, one hour before the witness was last seen.
Detective Marsh was running a surveillance operation across town from 9 PM until after midnight, confirmed by four colleagues.
cases.explanation: Victor had no access to the location. Detective Marsh has a confirmed alibi with multiple witnesses. Sara accessed the encrypted memo and made a call to an unregistered number one hour before the witness vanished. No other person with location knowledge is unaccounted for.
cases.hint: Only two people could know where the witness was. Check their movements that night.

cases.title: The Rooftop Drop
cases.intro: A prototype device was stolen from a research firm's rooftop testing area during an evening equipment check. The device requires specialist handling equipment to move. Access to the roof is restricted to three staff members.

suspects.name: Dr. Anita Cho
suspects.description: Lead researcher who designed the device and knows its handling specifications

suspects.name: Ben Harlow
suspects.description: Facilities manager, holds the only spare rooftop key and manages the lift schedule

suspects.name: Yusuf Ademi
suspects.description: Junior technician who assisted with the evening check before leaving

clues: The device was moved using the cargo lift, which requires a facilities staff keycard. Yusuf Ademi's keycard does not have lift clearance.
Dr. Cho left the building at 6:15 PM, confirmed by lobby camera. The theft occurred between 7:00 PM and 8:30 PM.
Ben Harlow's keycard was used to activate the cargo lift at 7:44 PM. He told investigators he had already gone home by 7:00 PM.
Yusuf Ademi signed out at 5:50 PM and his exit is confirmed by lobby footage.
cases.explanation: Yusuf lacked cargo lift clearance and his exit is confirmed before the theft window. Dr. Cho left at 6:15 PM, confirmed on camera. The cargo lift was activated at 7:44 PM using Ben Harlow's keycard, during a period he falsely claimed to have been at home.
cases.hint: The cargo lift can only be operated with a specific keycard. Check who used it.

cases.title: The Retirement Party
cases.intro: A retired police chief collapsed and died at his own retirement party. Toxicology found a lethal dose of a cardiac medication in his drink. He had not been prescribed any such medication. The drink was handed to him by one of three guests.

suspects.name: Frank Lido
suspects.description: Former colleague the chief had testified against in a misconduct hearing years ago

suspects.name: Agnes Torr
suspects.description: The chief's estranged sister, who stood to inherit under a life insurance policy

suspects.name: Roy Pemberton
suspects.description: Current department captain, recently passed over for a promotion the chief had influenced

clues: The medication found in the drink is a prescription compound. A pharmacy record shows it was filled under Agnes Torr's name two weeks before the party.
Frank Lido arrived at the party at 8:45 PM. The chief's drink was handed to him at 8:15 PM, confirmed by event photographs.
Roy Pemberton handed the chief his drink in a photograph timestamped 8:14 PM, but he told investigators he arrived at 8:30 PM.
Agnes Torr was photographed talking to other guests from 8:00 PM to 9:00 PM with no visible break.
cases.explanation: Agnes has photographic coverage of the relevant period. Frank was not present when the drink was handed over. Roy is photographed handing the chief the drink at 8:14 PM but claimed not to have arrived until 8:30 PM. The prescripted medication traces to Agnes, but she had no opportunity, while Roy's false alibi and photographic evidence place him directly at the point of poisoning.
cases.hint: Someone lied about when they arrived. The timestamp on a photograph shows otherwise.

cases.title: The Broken Window
cases.intro: A rare stamp collection was stolen from a private residence while the owner was traveling. Entry appeared to be through a broken rear window, but the glass fell outward, not inward.

suspects.name: Nick Carver
suspects.description: The owner's nephew, who had visited the house the week before and knew the layout

suspects.name: Donna Hass
suspects.description: House cleaner who had a spare key and access to the interior

suspects.name: Tom Eccles
suspects.description: Neighbor who reported the break-in to police

clues: Glass from the broken window fell onto the exterior patio, not into the room. This means the window was broken from inside, not outside.
Donna Hass's spare key was found in her bag. No key was used to enter; the door deadbolt was not engaged.
Tom Eccles reported the break-in the morning after it occurred and told police the back light was on when he saw it at 11 PM the night before.
Nick Carver's cell phone pinged a tower six blocks from the house at 10:47 PM on the night of the theft.
cases.explanation: The glass falling outward proves the window was broken from inside, meaning the thief was already in the house and staged a false entry point. Donna had a key but the deadbolt was not used, suggesting the person inside did not need a key. Nick's phone places him six blocks away at 10:47 PM, consistent with the timeframe, and his prior visit gave him knowledge of the interior and where to find the collection.
cases.hint: The direction the glass fell tells you something important about how entry actually happened.

cases.title: The Undated Letter
cases.intro: A prominent author was found dead in his study. An undated handwritten letter on his desk appeared to be a confession to financial fraud. Investigators questioned whether the letter was written by the author or planted after his death.

suspects.name: Miriam Cole
suspects.description: The author's editor, who had power of attorney and stood to benefit from the estate

suspects.name: James Whittle
suspects.description: The author's accountant, who the letter accused directly of orchestrating the fraud

suspects.name: Rosa Fenn
suspects.description: The author's domestic staff member who discovered the body

clues: A handwriting expert confirmed the letter was written by the author, but the ink type differs from any pen found in the study.
James Whittle was in another country at the time of death, confirmed by immigration records.
The author was left-handed. The letter shows right-handed pen pressure, inconsistent with his other known correspondence.
Miriam Cole had access to both the study and a collection of the author's documents she used for a biography project.
cases.explanation: James was out of the country. Rosa discovered the body but had no apparent motive and no access to the author's documents. The pen pressure inconsistency suggests the letter was traced or copied, not genuinely written. Miriam had access to the author's handwriting samples through the biography project and had the access needed to plant the letter before Rosa arrived.
cases.hint: The pen pressure does not match the author's dominant hand. Someone copied his handwriting.

cases.title: The Empty Locker
cases.intro: A professional cyclist's performance data drive was stolen from his locker at a training facility one day before a major doping review. The drive contained months of training records. Three people had locker room access during the relevant window.

suspects.name: Coach Renata
suspects.description: Head coach with a master locker key and a vested interest in the review outcome

suspects.name: Davide Costa
suspects.description: Rival cyclist who had accused the victim of doping the previous season

suspects.name: Lena Schreiber
suspects.description: Facility administrator, processed locker assignments and held a master key

clues: The locker was opened without force. Only two master keys exist: one held by Coach Renata, one by Lena Schreiber.
Facility entry logs show Davide Costa was not in the building at any point on the day of the theft.
Coach Renata's master key was signed out at 3:10 PM and returned at 3:55 PM. The theft window is between 3:00 PM and 4:00 PM.
Lena Schreiber's key was secured in the admin safe during the same window, confirmed by the safe's access log.
cases.explanation: Davide was not in the building. Lena's key was in the safe during the theft window. Coach Renata signed out the only available master key during the exact period the theft occurred and returned it 45 minutes later with no documented reason for using it.
cases.hint: Only one master key was outside secured storage during the theft window.

cases.title: The Flooded Basement
cases.intro: A bookkeeper for a small construction firm disappeared along with five years of payroll records. When police searched the company's basement archive, they found the filing cabinets flooded, destroying most of the physical records. Three employees had after-hours access.

suspects.name: Gary Oakes
suspects.description: Site supervisor whose name appears irregularly in altered payroll entries

suspects.name: Vera Huston
suspects.description: Bookkeeper's assistant who had been denied a pay increase three months prior

suspects.name: Nate Corrigan
suspects.description: Facilities technician responsible for the building's water and utilities systems

clues: The flooding was caused by a water main valve being manually opened. The valve is in a locked utility room; only Nate Corrigan holds the key.
Vera Huston's after-hours badge access was used to enter the building at 11:40 PM on the night the flood began.
Gary Oakes's name appears in 14 irregularly altered payroll entries. If the records are destroyed, the alterations cannot be verified.
Nate Corrigan was on medical leave and had submitted his key to reception the previous week per company leave policy.
cases.explanation: Nate had submitted his key before the incident, removing his access. Gary had motive but no documented access to the utility room or building after hours. Vera's badge shows her entering the building at 11:40 PM, the night the flooding started. The valve could be reached by anyone inside the building without the key once the utility room was otherwise accessible.
cases.hint: Check who was confirmed inside the building the night the flooding started.

cases.title: The Transfer Order
cases.intro: A mid-sized investment firm discovered that 300,000 had been moved to an offshore account via an internal transfer order. The order was processed under the credentials of the firm's senior compliance officer, who denies authorizing it.

suspects.name: Helen Marsh
suspects.description: Senior compliance officer whose credentials were used for the transfer

suspects.name: Declan Forde
suspects.description: IT administrator with access to the authentication system and credential records

suspects.name: Priya Oommen
suspects.description: Junior analyst who shared an office with the compliance officer

clues: The transfer was initiated from an internal workstation at 6:42 AM, before normal business hours. Card access logs show only Declan Forde entered the building before 7:00 AM that day.
Helen Marsh's login session that morning began at 8:55 AM, confirmed by the authentication server.
Priya Oommen does not have system privileges to initiate transfers above 10,000.
Declan Forde has read-write access to stored authentication tokens and had resolved a ticket involving Helen Marsh's credentials the previous week.
cases.explanation: Priya lacked the system privilege level to initiate the transfer. Helen's authenticated session began more than two hours after the transfer was processed. Declan was the only person in the building at 6:42 AM, had prior access to Helen's credential tokens, and possessed the technical ability to use a stored token without her knowledge.
cases.hint: The transfer happened before most people were in the building. Check who was there.

cases.title: The Staged Accident
cases.intro: A wealthy collector died when the elevator in his private estate failed, dropping him two floors. An engineer's inspection found that the elevator's safety brake had been manually disengaged. Three people were in the estate on the day of the accident.

suspects.name: Anton Weiss
suspects.description: The collector's business partner, owed a disputed sum from a recent deal

suspects.name: Cora Webb
suspects.description: Estate housekeeper with twelve years of service and full knowledge of the building

suspects.name: Felix Drummond
suspects.description: Elevator maintenance technician who had serviced the system two days prior

clues: The safety brake requires a specific hex key to disengage. Only Felix Drummond's company supplies that tool as part of their maintenance kit.
Felix Drummond's service report from two days prior confirms the brake was functioning correctly at the time of his visit.
Cora Webb was photographed at a family event 40 kilometers away on the day of the incident, timestamps confirmed.
Anton Weiss was in the estate that morning and later stated he had never been near the elevator room. Staff saw him on that floor at 10:15 AM.
cases.explanation: Cora's alibi is confirmed by timestamped photographs. Felix confirmed the brake was working at his service visit, meaning it was tampered with afterward. The hex key Felix's company uses could have been taken from his kit. Anton was seen on the floor containing the elevator room despite claiming otherwise, and had financial motivation to cause the death.
cases.hint: The brake was fine two days ago. Someone tampered with it after the last service visit.

cases.title: The Archive Key
cases.intro: Classified blueprints from a defense contractor went missing from a locked archive room. An internal investigation traced the leak to a photo taken on a mobile device inside the room. Three employees had authorized archive access.

suspects.name: Richard Lam
suspects.description: Senior engineer with archive access and a personal financial dispute with the firm

suspects.name: Toni Baxter
suspects.description: Document control officer responsible for the archive, recently headhunted by a competitor

suspects.name: Omar Nassif
suspects.description: Junior engineer who applied for archive access three months ago and was denied

clues: Omar Nassif's access application was denied. He does not have an archive keycard.
The metadata on the leaked photograph shows it was taken at 7:52 AM on a Tuesday. Toni Baxter's timesheet shows she started work at 9:00 AM on that day.
Richard Lam's keycard was used to access the archive at 7:49 AM on the same Tuesday.
The phone model in the metadata matches the make of phone issued to Richard Lam by the company.
cases.explanation: Omar had no keycard. Toni did not arrive until 9:00 AM, over an hour after the photo was taken. Richard's keycard accessed the archive three minutes before the photograph was taken, and the phone metadata matches his company-issued device.
cases.hint: The photo timestamp and archive entry log point to the same three-minute window.

cases.title: The Night Porter
cases.intro: A hotel guest was found dead in his room during the early morning hours. He had been struck with a blunt object that was not recovered. The hotel's corridor camera was offline between 2:00 AM and 3:20 AM. Three staff members were on duty.

suspects.name: Alec Dorn
suspects.description: Night porter responsible for corridor security and guest requests on that floor

suspects.name: Greta Woll
suspects.description: Front desk clerk, stationed at reception the entire shift per standard procedure

suspects.name: Jerome Falk
suspects.description: Maintenance worker called in for an emergency boiler issue in the basement

clues: The guest's room key card log shows the door opened at 2:24 AM using a master passcard. Only night porters carry master passcards during shifts.
Greta Woll's login activity on the front desk terminal is continuous from midnight to 4:00 AM with no extended gaps.
Jerome Falk's maintenance log and the boiler system's digital record confirm he was working in the basement between 1:55 AM and 3:40 AM.
Alec Dorn told investigators he was on his routine floor check between 2:00 AM and 3:00 AM and had no other calls logged.
cases.explanation: Greta's terminal activity shows continuous presence at the desk. Jerome's work is confirmed by the boiler system's own digital log. The room was opened with a master passcard during the period the corridor camera was offline. Alec was the only night porter on that floor, carried the relevant passcard, and has no corroborated account of his movements during the exact window.
cases.hint: The room opened with a master passcard that only one category of staff carries.

cases.title: The Proxy Vote
cases.intro: A shareholder vote at a private company was manipulated when proxy forms for three absent shareholders were submitted fraudulently. The forms changed the outcome of a major acquisition vote. Access to the shareholders' personal files was required to forge the forms.

suspects.name: Irene Toft
suspects.description: Company secretary with administrative access to all shareholder records

suspects.name: Malcolm Birch
suspects.description: Acquisition deal advisor hired externally for this transaction

suspects.name: Stuart Pace
suspects.description: Majority shareholder who would benefit if the acquisition passed

clues: The proxy forms required personal details from each shareholder's file. Malcolm Birch was not granted access to shareholder records under his advisory contract.
Stuart Pace voted in person and would have needed the acquisition to pass, but his own proxy was not affected.
The shareholder record system's access log shows a query pulling all three affected shareholders' contact and signature data at 4:15 PM, three days before the vote.
The 4:15 PM query was made using Irene Toft's administrator credentials.
cases.explanation: Malcolm lacked system access. Stuart voted in person and had no reason to forge others' proxies since his own vote was already counted. The signature data needed to forge the forms was pulled from the system using Irene's credentials at a specific time three days before the vote, tying her directly to the preparation of the forgery.
cases.hint: Whoever forged the forms needed the shareholders' signature data first.

cases.title: The Open Window
cases.intro: A senior scientist at a pharmaceutical research facility was found unconscious in her office with traces of sedative in her coffee. Her personal research notes, which represented three years of unpublished work, were gone.

suspects.name: Dr. Paulo Reis
suspects.description: Research rival within the same facility who had filed two patent disputes against the victim

suspects.name: Carmen Vidal
suspects.description: Lab technician who prepared the coffee cart each morning and distributed drinks to offices

suspects.name: Sven Lund
suspects.description: Visiting researcher from a partner institution who had toured the facility that day

clues: The sedative found in the coffee is stored in a locked refrigerator in the main lab. Only Carmen Vidal and the lab manager have keys to that refrigerator.
Sven Lund's visitor pass restricts him to the conference wing. He did not have building access to any lab or office corridor.
Dr. Paulo Reis's key card shows he entered the facility at 11:30 AM. The sedative was introduced before 9:00 AM, when the victim drank her first coffee.
Carmen Vidal prepared and distributed the coffee cart between 8:15 AM and 8:50 AM, with exclusive access to the coffee before distribution.
cases.explanation: Sven could not access the relevant areas. Dr. Reis did not arrive until hours after the sedative was introduced. Only Carmen and the lab manager could access the sedative supply, and Carmen was the one with direct contact with the coffee before it reached the victim.
cases.hint: Two people had access to the sedative. Only one had contact with the coffee before it was delivered.
